# X2Chess User Manual

Last updated: 2026-04-20

## Who this manual is for

This manual is for people using the X2Chess application to open, navigate,
edit, and study chess games in PGN format.

---

## Application layout

X2Chess has three main areas:

- **Left: Resource viewer** — browse and open games from your collection.
- **Centre: Board and editor** — the chess board and PGN text editor side by side.
- **Right: Analysis panels** — engine analysis, opening explorer, tablebase,
  and search panels stacked vertically.
- **Top: Menu bar** — session tabs, game info, and the menu icon.

---

## Opening and managing games

### Opening a game from your collection

1. The resource viewer (left panel) shows your game databases and files.
2. Click a game row to open it in the centre editor.
3. The game opens as a new tab in the session tab strip at the top.

### Opening a game by drag-and-drop or paste

- **Drag** one or more `.pgn` files onto the app window to open them.
- **Paste** PGN text anywhere in the app (`Cmd/Ctrl + V`) to open it as a new
  game.

### Working with multiple games

- Open games appear as tabs in the tab strip above the board.
- Click a tab to switch to that game. Each game maintains its own position,
  undo history, and editor state.
- Close a tab with the × button on the tab.

### Creating a new game

- Click **+ New game** on the same row as the open resource tabs in the resource viewer.
- The New Game dialog opens. Choose:
  - **Standard** — starts from the normal starting position.
  - **Custom FEN** — enter any FEN string to start from a specific position.
    Use the **information** button beside the FEN field for a short guide to
    each FEN field (opens after a brief hover or when clicked).
  - **Chess960** — tick the Chess960 checkbox to generate a random starting
    position following Chess960 rules.
- Click **Create** to open the new game in the editor.

### Importing games from other formats

X2Chess can import game files in formats other than PGN. The importer converts
the source file into games stored in your local collection.

#### EPD (Extended Position Description)

EPD files (`.epd`) contain one chess position per line. Each position is
imported as a separate game starting from that position:

- The `id` field in the EPD record becomes the **Event** header.
- The `bm` (best move) field is recorded in the **Annotator** header.
- Comment fields (`c0`–`c9`) and centipawn evaluations (`ce`) appear as a
  comment after the position.
- Blank lines and `#` comment lines are skipped.

EPD import is available in all builds (browser and desktop).

#### ChessBase databases (CBH, CBV)

ChessBase `.cbh` and `.cbv` files can be imported on **desktop** (Tauri)
builds. The app passes the file to the backend parser and converts all games
to PGN for storage in your local `.x2chess` database.

- `.cbh` — the main ChessBase database file (entry point for a CBH set).
- `.cbv` — a compressed ChessBase archive containing a complete database.

::: {.callout-note}
ChessBase import requires the Tauri desktop build. It is not available in the
browser version.
:::

---

## Game info and metadata

The game info bar sits above the board. It shows the players, event, date, and
opening line for the active game.

- Use the **▼** control on the game info card to open the full header editor.
- Standard tags (players, ratings, event, date, result, …) are editable there.
- **XSqr head** shows the main line as **moves only** (move numbers and piece
  moves, no comments or symbols) up to the first variation or the end of the main
  line. It is read-only in the UI (you can select and copy the text); when you save
  the game, that text is stored in the PGN tag `[XSqrHead "..."]`.
- Metadata fields in lists also depend on the active schema (see **Metadata schemas** below).
- The **Result** field accepts `1-0`, `0-1`, `½-½`, or `*`.

---

## Navigating a game

### Toolbar navigation buttons

| Button | Action | Keyboard |
|---|---|---|
| \|< | Go to start | — |
| < | Go to previous move | Arrow Left |
| > | Go to next move | Arrow Right |
| >\| | Go to end | — |

### Keyboard shortcuts in the editor

| Key | Action |
|---|---|
| Arrow Left | Previous move |
| Arrow Right | Next move |
| Arrow Down | Enter first variation |
| Arrow Left (at variation start) | Return to parent move |
| Shift + Arrow Down (at variation start) | Cycle to next sibling variation |
| Shift + Arrow Up (at variation start) | Cycle to previous sibling variation |
| Shift + Arrow Left | Jump to comment before selected move |
| Shift + Arrow Right | Jump to comment after selected move |
| Cmd/Ctrl + Z | Undo |
| Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + Z  or  Cmd/Ctrl + Y | Redo |

---

## The PGN text editor

The text editor is the primary surface for reading and editing game notation.

### What you see

- **Mainline moves** are shown prominently (bold).
- **Variations** are nested with lighter visual weight.
- **Comments** appear inline in the text flow.
- The **selected move** is highlighted; selecting a move updates the board.

### PGN layout modes

Use the **Plain / Text / Tree** buttons in the toolbar to switch layout:

- **Plain** — compact, literal PGN notation, close to the raw file format.
  Comments are shown verbatim; no formatting markers are interpreted.
- **Text** — narrative layout with line breaks and indentation for readability.
  The `[[br]]` marker inside a comment inserts a visible line break. Variation
  blocks are indented structurally by variation depth (including comments between
  their moves).
- **Tree** — structured view emphasising variation branching.  Each variation
  appears as its own block, opened by a labelled pill button (e.g. **A**, **B.1**).
  Formatting markers are shown as greyed literal text rather than applied.

### The intro section

In **Text** and **Tree** mode, the comment that appears *before the very first
move* of a game (or variation) is automatically styled as the **intro section**
— a visually distinct block with a sidebar accent and a tinted background.
This makes it easy to write an introductory paragraph about a game or variation:
just put the comment before the first move and it will be highlighted.

No special markup is needed — the position in the notation is all that matters.
If the first comment comes *after* the first move it is treated as a normal
comment.

In Tree mode every variation branch gets its own intro comment independently,
so you can open each branch with a short description.

**Example PGN** — the opening comment automatically becomes the intro:
```
[White "Kasparov"]
[Black "Deep Blue"]

{ This game was the decisive encounter of the 1997 match. }
1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 ...
```

### Formatting marker (`[[br]]`)

Only `[[br]]` is interpreted as a layout marker in **Text** mode. In **Tree**
mode, marker text remains literal.

| Marker | Effect |
|---|---|
| `[[br]]` | Inserts a line break at that point in the comment (Text mode). |

### Adding and editing comments

1. Select a move in the editor.
2. Use the toolbar buttons to add a comment:
   - **Insert comment left** — adds a comment before the selected move.
   - **Insert comment right** — adds a comment after the selected move.
3. Click into a comment and type to edit it directly.
4. Leaving a comment empty removes it automatically.
5. Newly inserted comments are briefly highlighted.

### Move action popup

- Clicking a half-move opens a small popup with **insert comment before**, **Q/A**, and **insert comment after** actions.
- The same popup also includes **Insert null move after** to add a single `--` move after the selected move.
- For a null move (`--`), the same popup also provides **Delete this null move** to remove only that single null move.
- If a null move has exactly one continuation variation and no competing continuation, deleting that null move merges that continuation back into the mainline automatically.
- The popup is rendered above nearby move tokens and comment text in both **Text** and **Tree** layouts.

### Move symbols and evaluation symbols (NAG picker)

Selecting a move in the editor shows a **symbol toolbar** above the editor
content with three rows of annotation buttons:

| Row | Symbols | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Move quality | ! &nbsp; ? &nbsp; !! &nbsp; ?? &nbsp; !? &nbsp; ?! | Good, mistake, brilliant, blunder, interesting, dubious |
| Evaluation | = &nbsp; ∞ &nbsp; ⩲ &nbsp; ⩱ &nbsp; ± &nbsp; ∓ &nbsp; +- &nbsp; -+ | Equal, unclear, slight/clear/winning advantage for each side |
| Positional | → &nbsp; ↑ &nbsp; ⇆ &nbsp; ⊠ &nbsp; □ &nbsp; △ &nbsp; ⊞ &nbsp; N &nbsp; =/∞ | Initiative, attack, counterplay, zugzwang, weak point, with the idea, better was, novelty, compensation |

**How to use:**

- Click any button to attach that symbol to the selected move. The symbol
  appears immediately after the move in the editor.
- Click an active (highlighted) symbol again to remove it.
- Within each row, only one symbol can be active at a time — selecting a
  different symbol in the same row replaces the previous one.
- Symbols from different rows are independent and can all be set simultaneously.
- The **→**, **↑**, and **⇆** positional symbols automatically use the
  correct colour-specific code based on whose move it is — no manual selection
  needed.
- Symbol changes are part of the undo/redo history.

Symbols are stored as standard NAG codes in the PGN (`$1`, `$2`, etc.) and
are fully compatible with other chess software.

### Formatting within comments (Text and Tree modes)

In Text and Tree layout modes, comments support explicit line breaks:

- `[[br]]` — inserts a line break at that point in the narrative.

Use the toolbar button **Insert line break** to insert this marker at the
cursor position. Variation indentation is automatic and follows the move tree.

### Editor style options for comments and variations

Open **Menu → Editor style** to configure PGN editor rendering:

- **Comment line breaks**:
  - **Mainline only** — comments on the mainline start a new line; variation comments stay inline.
  - **Always** — every comment starts on a new line.
- **Variation move colour** — changes move text colour for variation moves.
- **Comment text colour** — changes colour for comment text in text/tree modes.

### Q/A annotations

Moves can carry Q/A (question/answer) annotations used by Study mode. A badge
on the move token indicates a Q/A annotation is present. To add or edit one,
right-click a move token or use the Q/A button in the editor context menu.

---

## The chess board

### Making moves (move entry)

When no engine game is active, you can enter moves directly on the board:

1. Click or drag a piece to a destination square.
2. If the move is unambiguous, it is played immediately.
3. If the move is ambiguous (e.g. two rooks can reach the same square), the
   **Disambiguation dialog** opens — choose the correct piece.
4. If a pawn reaches the back rank, the **Promotion picker** appears — choose
   the promotion piece.
5. The move is added to the game at the current position. If you are not at the
   end of the game, a new variation is created.

### Board orientation

Use the **Flip board** button (below the board) to swap which side plays from
the bottom.

### Move destination hints

Hover the mouse over any piece to see small dots appear on every square it can
legally move to.  Squares that contain a capturable enemy piece show a ring
instead of a dot.  The dots disappear as soon as you move the mouse away.

### Board annotations — highlights and arrows

You can draw coloured highlights and arrows on the board using the right mouse
button.  Annotations are saved into the game's PGN and restored whenever you
navigate back to the same position.

| Gesture | Effect |
|---------|--------|
| Right-click a square | Toggle a **green** highlight on that square |
| Shift + right-click a square | Toggle a **red** highlight on that square |
| Right-click drag to another square | Toggle a **green** arrow |
| Shift + right-click drag | Toggle a **red** arrow |
| Right-click an existing highlight or arrow origin | **Remove** that shape |
| Right-click an empty area of the board | **Clear all** shapes for the current move |

Clicking or dragging again on an existing shape of the same colour removes it
(toggle behaviour).

Annotations are stored in the PGN using the standard `[%csl]` (squares) and
`[%cal]` (arrows) comment extensions, so they are compatible with other chess
software.

### Best-move hint

Click the **Hint** button in the toolbar to show an arrow on the board
indicating the engine's recommended move. The hint clears automatically when
you advance to the next move.

### Editing the starting position

For games that start from a custom position:

1. Click **Position** in the toolbar (visible when the game has a custom FEN).
2. The **Edit Starting Position** dialog opens — drag pieces to set up the
   desired position, or enter a FEN string directly.
3. Click **Confirm** to update the game's starting position.

### Extracting the current position

To create a new game starting from the current board position:

1. Click **Extract** in the toolbar.
2. The **Extract Position** dialog confirms the FEN.
3. Click **Extract** to open a new game session from that position.

---

## Saving

- The **Save** button in the toolbar is highlighted when the game has unsaved
  changes.
- Click **Save** (or press `Cmd/Ctrl + S`) to save to the source file or database.
- Undo and redo are available with the toolbar buttons or keyboard shortcuts.

---

## Engine analysis

The **Analysis panel** is in the right column.

### Starting and stopping analysis

- Click **Start** in the Analysis panel to begin real-time analysis of the
  current position.
- Click **Stop** to halt analysis.
- Analysis restarts automatically when you navigate to a new move while it is
  running.

### Reading the analysis

- The panel shows the top engine lines (variations), each with:
  - **Score** — evaluation in pawns (positive = white advantage).
  - **Depth** — search depth reached.
  - **Principal variation** — the sequence of best moves found.
- Click a move in an engine line to preview that position on the board.

### Play vs engine

1. Click **vs Engine** in the toolbar.
2. The **Play vs Engine** dialog opens — choose your colour and time control.
3. Click **Start** to begin. The engine plays the opposite colour.
4. Make your moves on the board. The engine responds automatically.
5. Click **Stop** (toolbar button turns to "Stop" while a game is active) to
   end the game at any time.

### Annotate game with engine

1. Click **Annotate** in the toolbar.
2. The **Annotate Game** dialog opens — configure the engine depth and which
   moves to annotate.
3. Click **Run** to process the game. Engine evaluations and best moves are
   added as comments.

---

## Opening explorer

The **Opening Explorer panel** is in the right column, below the Analysis
panel.

- Toggle it on/off with the switch in the panel header.
- Switch between **Masters** (master-level OTB games) and **Lichess** (online
  games) databases using the source selector.
- The panel shows moves played from the current position, with frequency and
  win-rate statistics.
- Click a move in the explorer to play it on the board.
- Configure ratings and time controls via **Settings** (⚙ icon in the panel
  header).

---

## Tablebase

The **Tablebase panel** is in the right column, below the Opening Explorer.

- Toggle it on/off with the switch in the panel header.
- When the position has 7 or fewer pieces, the panel shows the tablebase
  result (Win / Draw / Loss) and the best move with DTZ (distance to zeroing).
- Click a move in the tablebase result to play it on the board.

---

## Study mode

Study mode presents Q/A annotation pairs as interactive prompts.

### Requirements

The game must contain at least one Q/A annotation (see **Q/A annotations**
above). The **Study** toolbar button is enabled only when annotations are
present.

### Running a study session

1. Click **Study** in the toolbar.
2. A prompt overlay appears on the board showing the question from the first
   annotation.
3. Make the move on the board that you think is the answer.
4. The overlay reveals whether your move was correct and shows the annotated
   answer.
5. Click **Next** to advance to the next Q/A item.
6. The session ends when all items have been shown.

---

## Training mode

Training mode replays moves from the game to test your recall.

### Starting a training session

1. Click **Train** in the toolbar.
2. The **Training Launcher** dialog opens. Choose a protocol:
   - **Replay** — play through the game move by move from memory.
   - **Opening** — drill the opening moves specifically.
3. Click **Start**.

### During training

The board shows the current position. You are expected to play the move that
was played in the game — or any move the game's annotator has judged as equally
good.

#### Move outcomes

| Indicator | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Correct** | You played the game move or a recognised strong alternative. |
| **Better!** | You found an improvement over a flawed game move. |
| **Correct** *(note)* | You played the game move, but a better option existed. Training continues. |
| **Dubious** | Your move is in the game's analysis but considered questionable. Depending on settings, training may continue or ask you to find a better move. |
| **Wrong** | Your move was not accepted. The best move is revealed. |

#### Hints

Click **Hint** in the training overlay to reveal the canonical best move for
the current position. Using a hint reduces your final score for that move.
The number of hints available is shown in the overlay.

#### Skipping a move

Click **Skip** to advance without playing. Skipped moves are excluded from
your score but are listed in the result summary.

#### Session end

When all moves have been attempted, the result summary shows your score,
a per-move breakdown, and merge options (see "Merging training results").

---

### Annotating games for training

X2Chess reads standard PGN annotation conventions when evaluating your moves.
Richer annotation produces better training feedback.

#### Move symbols

Attach move symbols to individual moves using the NAG buttons in the editor
(**!** **!!** **?** **??** **!?** **?!**). The training engine uses them as
follows:

| Symbol | Meaning for training |
|---|---|
| !! — Brilliant | This move is the canonical answer. |
| ! — Good | This move is the canonical answer. |
| !? — Interesting | This move is the canonical answer; training notes its speculative nature. |
| ?! — Dubious | This is the game move; training notes that a better option may exist. |
| ? — Mistake | This move is **not** the canonical answer. Add a variation to show the correct move. |
| ?? — Blunder | As above. The variation's first move, if annotated !, becomes the expected answer. |

#### Variations as alternatives

Any variation (RAV) added immediately after a move is read by the training
engine:

- A variation starting with **!** or **!!** is accepted as a strong alternative
  (full credit).
- A variation starting with **!?** is accepted as a speculative alternative
  (full credit).
- A variation with **no symbol** is accepted as a neutral alternative
  (full credit).
- A variation starting with **?!** marks a dubious option (partial credit, or
  a prompted retry, depending on settings).
- A variation starting with **?** or **??** is treated as a trap line: if the
  user plays that move, they receive the variation's comment as feedback
  explaining why it fails.

**Example:** The game continued 24. Nf3? — a mistake. Add a variation
`(24. Nd5! with the comment "Centralises the knight and controls e7")`.
The training engine expects Nd5, rewards players who find it with "Better!"
feedback, and shows the comment as explanation.

#### Engine evaluations

If you annotate a game using the engine, the resulting `[%eval]` values are
used as a quantitative fallback when a move has no symbol or variation. Moves
within 0.3 pawns of the position evaluation are accepted; moves 0.3–0.8 pawns
worse are treated as dubious; moves more than 0.8 pawns worse are rejected.
These thresholds can be adjusted in **Training Settings**.

#### The `[%train]` tag (advanced)

For precise control over a specific position, embed a `[%train]` tag in the
comment *before* the move. This overrides all other rules for that ply.

```
{[%train accept="g1f3,d1h5" reject="g1h3" hint="Activate the knight"]}
24. Nf3
```

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `accept` | Comma-separated UCI moves that count as correct. The game move is included unless listed in `reject`. |
| `reject` | UCI moves that trigger a specific trap warning (taken from the matching variation's comment). |
| `hint` | Text shown when the user requests a hint at this position. |

Use `[%train]` when NAG/variation inference is insufficient, or when you need
to accept a set of moves that cannot be expressed as variations.

---

## Resource viewer

The resource viewer (left panel) lets you browse, filter, and group your game
collection.

### Browsing

- Games and collections appear as rows in the table.
- Click a row to open that game.
- The viewer supports multiple tabs for different open collections.
- Saving a new game into an open resource refreshes matching resource-table rows automatically.
- Reordering games in an open resource refreshes matching resource-table rows automatically.
- Drag a column handle (⠿) in the table header to reorder columns.
- Column headers stay aligned horizontally with the data columns while sorting, filtering, and resizing.

### Grouping

Use the **Group by** toolbar above the table to organise rows into collapsible
groups:

1. Click the **+ Add level** dropdown and select a field (e.g. Event, White,
   Result).
2. The table regroups immediately.
3. Add further levels for nested grouping.
4. Use the **↑** button on a pill to promote that level. Use **×** to remove
   it.
5. When no grouping is active, the toolbar shows **none** next to **Group by:**.
6. Click **Clear** to remove all grouping. The button is always visible and is
   disabled when no group is active.

### Filtering

- Click a column header value to filter by that value.
- Active filters are shown above the table.
- Click **Clear filters** to remove all active filters.

### Metadata schemas

A schema defines which metadata fields appear in the game info bar and the
resource table.

- The **Schema** selector in the resource toolbar switches the active schema.
- For **Standard PGN**, default table columns list White first, then Black, then
  other tags; **Result** appears before **ECO**. The resource type “Source” is
  not offered as a column.
- Use **Add metadata…** (dropdown next to **Manage schemas…**) to append any
  field from the full **known** PGN list (including X2 tags such as style,
  `Material`, `XSqrHead`) plus any extra header keys discovered in loaded games.
  Entries are sorted **alphabetically** by name. Columns are inserted in canonical order next to related tags.
  Click **×** on a column header (except **Game**) to remove that column from the table.
- Click **Manage schemas…** to open the **Schema Editor**:
  - Add, rename, or remove custom schemas.
  - For each schema, define fields: name, type (text, number, flag, select),
    and optional default value.
  - Schemas are saved locally and persist across sessions.

---

## Menu

Click the **☰** icon in the top-right corner to open the app menu.

- **Developer Tools** toggle — enables the Developer Dock and other diagnostic
  panels.
- **Save mode** — switch between Autosave and Manual save for the active game.
- Additional app-level settings appear here as features are added.

Close the menu with the close button, `Esc`, or by clicking outside it.

---

## Developer tools

Enable **Developer Tools** in the menu to access diagnostic panels.

### Developer Dock

Click **Open Developer Dock** (or use the toggle in the menu) to open a
fixed bottom dock with two tabs:

- **AST** — the internal parse tree for the current PGN.
- **Raw PGN** — the current game as PGN text. You can edit the text and click
  **Apply to session** to replace the open game everywhere in the app (board,
  editor, navigation). This updates only memory: it does **not** save to disk
  automatically, and it clears undo history for that session.
- The Raw PGN tab also shows a **PGN quality** panel:
  - **OK** (green pill) — PGN is fully supported.
  - **Warning** (yellow pill) — issues exist, but the app can still use the PGN with recovery.
  - **Critical** (red pill) — PGN cannot be parsed reliably and may not be usable.
- When strict parsing fails, the panel lists detected issues with line/column
  hints so you can locate non-standard or faulty notation quickly.
- Use **Copy diagnostics** to copy the current status and issue list to your
  clipboard for bug reports or external review.
- Click an issue line to jump to and highlight the relevant line in the Raw PGN editor.
- Use **Preview auto-fix** to inspect a before/after preview, then click
  **Apply fixes** to run safe PGN compatibility repairs (legacy X2 header
  renames and missing `SetUp` insertion for FEN games).

The dock always reflects the same game and position as the main editor after you apply.

---

## Keyboard shortcuts summary

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| Arrow Left / Right | Navigate moves |
| Arrow Down | Enter variation |
| Shift + Arrow Down/Up (at variation start) | Cycle sibling variations |
| Shift + Arrow Left/Right | Jump to comment |
| Cmd/Ctrl + Z | Undo |
| Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + Z | Redo |
| Cmd/Ctrl + S | Save |
| Cmd/Ctrl + V | Paste PGN |
| Esc | Close dialogs / menu |
